title: Netflix's GenPage generates an entire personalized homepage with one transformer
author: Lequn Wang, Jiangwei Pan, and Linas Baltrunas
tldr: GenPage replaces Netflix's multi-stage homepage recommender with a decoder-only transformer that improved engagement and cut end-to-end serving latency by 20%.
summary:
We rebuilt homepage construction as one generative task: given a user's history, profile, request context, and the page generated so far, **GenPage** autoregressively chooses the next row or entity. That matters because a Netflix homepage is not one ranked list; it is a two-dimensional layout whose rows and titles interact. A Continue Watching row may satisfy immediate intent but reduce browsing, so optimizing each candidate independently misses whole-page effects.

We serialize both sides into domain-specific tokens. The prompt contains actions, profile attributes, time, device, and other request signals; the response contains row and entity IDs in layout order. A custom tokenizer is dramatically leaner than text tokenization: an event such as watching a title for 50 minutes 30 days ago takes four structured tokens instead of 16 GPT-5 tokens. The direct mapping also lets product rules operate on rows and titles rather than ambiguous text spans.

The model is a decoder-only transformer trained with an LLM-like recipe. Next-token pretraining teaches the language of successful production homepages. Post-training then either uses **weighted binary classification**, turning feedback magnitude and sign into token-level value targets, or reinforcement learning against a page-level reward model. RL can capture interactions across the whole page and naturally supports multi-token entities, but it needs a KL penalty to keep generation inside the reward model's reliable distribution and limit reward hacking.

Production constraints shaped the design as much as model quality. New titles combine an ID embedding with semantic signals from synopses, cast, transcripts, genres, and video, while fallback tokens and context injection cover cold start. Large training passes alternate with daily incremental updates mixed with historical samples to stay fresh without catastrophic forgetting. Constrained decoding enforces deduplication, pinning, eligibility, and row consistency, and hybrid row decoding generates the most visible items autoregressively before filling the rest in one pass.

The experiments challenge the instinct to scale parameters first. Growing the model from roughly **120M to 900M parameters** reduced weighted-classification loss by about **1.3%**, while cumulative improvements to prompt context reduced it by about **6.9%**. In this regime, giving the model better information and representations mattered more than a 7.5× increase in capacity. RL also increased page diversity even though diversity was not explicitly rewarded, suggesting genuine whole-page optimization.

In a 14-day online A/B test against Netflix's mature production stack, every GenPage variant produced a statistically significant lift in the core engagement metric, with **p < 0.001**. The system reacted quickly to in-session actions, then faded back toward long-term preferences after a day or two. It also cut end-to-end latency by **20%** by collapsing ranking stages and heavy feature computation into one model. Category-distribution shifts still need investigation, and long context still depends partly on handcrafted summaries, but the result shows that an end-to-end generative recommender can be both better and faster in production.

media.alt: Animation showing GenPage consuming user context and generating homepage rows and titles one step at a time.
media.caption: GenPage treats user context as a prompt and generates the structured homepage autoregressively.

media.alt: Diagram of context tokens for user history, profile, and request data alongside page tokens for rows and entity IDs.
media.caption: Domain-specific tokenization keeps sequences compact and maps product rules directly to tokens.

media.alt: Line chart showing positive treatment effects for five GenPage variants over a 14-day observation window.
media.caption: All tested GenPage variants beat the production baseline on Netflix's launch engagement metric.

keyPoints: GenPage generates rows, entities, and layout together instead of coordinating several independently optimized ranking stages.
Custom tokens compress user events and make cold-start handling, business rules, and constrained decoding tractable.
Prompt enrichment cut offline loss by about 6.9%, versus about 1.3% from scaling parameters roughly 7.5×.
Weighted binary classification offers simple token-level credit assignment; RL enables direct page-level optimization.
Online tests improved core engagement with p < 0.001 and reduced end-to-end serving latency by 20%.

quiz.question: What does GenPage generate jointly?
options: Rows, entities, and their page layout
quiz.explanation: GenPage models the homepage as a structured response containing both rows and the entities inside them.

quiz.question: Why does Netflix use a domain-specific tokenizer?
options: To translate titles into every language
To shorten sequences and map product concepts directly to tokens
quiz.explanation: Structured tokens reduce inference cost and let rules target exact rows and entities.

quiz.question: Which change reduced weighted-classification loss more in the reported offline studies?
quiz.explanation: Context enrichment yielded about a 6.9% reduction, compared with about 1.3% from model scaling.

quiz.question: What happened in the production A/B test?
options: The model was slower than the existing stack
quiz.explanation: All tested variants significantly improved the launch metric while cutting end-to-end serving latency by 20%.
